page.content: The data presented in the accompanying table provides a comprehensive overview of the distribution of MPO and IL-17 expression within the study cohort, highlighting significant differences in demographic characteristics, particularly age. The median age of participants in the MPO-/IL17-n group was 62 years, with a range spanning from 39 to 77 years, indicating a relatively older demographic compared to the other groups. In contrast, the MPO+/IL17-n group exhibited a slightly younger median age of 59.5 years, with a narrower range of 50 to 65 years. This trend continues with the MPO-/IL17+n and MPO+/IL17+n groups, which have median ages of 57 years (34–72) and 55 years (45–73), respectively.

lines.text: The data presented in the accompanying table provides a comprehensive overview of the distribution of MPO and IL-17
expression within the study cohort, highlighting significant differences in demographic characteristics, particularly age. The
median age of participants in the MPO-/IL17-n group was 62 years, with a range spanning from 39 to 77 years, indicating a
relatively older demographic compared to the other groups. In contrast, the MPO+/IL17-n group exhibited a slightly younger
median age of 59.5 years, with a narrower range of 50 to 65 years. This trend continues with the MPO-/IL17+n and
MPO+/IL17+n groups, which have median ages of 57 years (34–72) and 55 years (45–73), respectively.

page.content: These findings suggest a potential correlation between MPO and IL-17 expression and the age of subjects, warranting further investigation into the underlying biological mechanisms. Moreover, the p-value associated with these age distributions could provide insights into the statistical significance of the observed differences across the groups, emphasizing the importance of age as a potential confounder in studies focused on inflammatory markers. This contextual understanding is crucial for interpreting the implications of MPO and IL-17 expression in relation to age-related health outcomes.
